---
title: "Lab 7: T-Tests and Plotting 2 or more variables"
author:
- name: Tom Hanna
  id: th
  orcid: 0000-0002-8054-0335
  email: tlhanna@central.uh.edu
  url: tomhanna.me
  affiliation:
  - name: University of Houston
    city: Houston
    state: TX
    url: uh.edu/class/political-science/
license: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0
copyright:
  holder: Tom Hanna
  year: 2026
date: today
format:
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    style: assets/custom.css
    axe: true
    self-contained: true
    code-fold: false
    logo: assets/logo.png
    footer: "POLS3316, Spring 2026, Instructor: Tom Hanna"
  revealjs:
    self-contained: true
    transition: convex
    incremental: true
    theme: assets/custom.scss
    slide-number: true
    preview-links: true
    logo: assets/logo.png
    footer: "UH POLS3316, Spring 2026, Instructor: Tom Hanna"
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    fig_caption: true
from: markdown-smart
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
  warning: false
  message: false
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    tidy: true
    tidy.opts:
      width.cutoff: 70
---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| eval: true
#| label: setup

library(here)
here::i_am("Labs/Lab_7/lab_7.qmd")
```

## Getting ready

### Proper project

Be sure you are in the project for the class github repository. You can check this by looking at the top of the RStudio window, it should show the name of the project as `"pols3316_spring2026"`. If not, you can open the project by going to `File > Open Project` and selecting the `pols3316_spring2026.Rproj` file in the folder where you cloned the repository or by going to `File > Recent Projects` and selecting it from the list.

### Clear the console and environment

Next, we are going to clear the console and remove all objects from the environment.

To clear the console, you can use the shortcut `Ctrl + L` (Windows/Linux) or `Cmd + L` (Mac).

To clear the environment, you can use the broom icon in the upper right or use following command in R by typing it in the console:

```r
# this command erases all objects from the environment

rm(list = ls())

```

## Git Pull

Last, we are going to do a "Git Pull" to update the local repository with the latest material from the class github repository on GitHub.

You can do this by going to the "Git" tab in the upper right pane of RStudio and clicking the "Pull" button or by using the following command in R by typing it in the console:

```
# this executes a git pull

system("git pull")
``` 


Now, let's create a new R script file for this lab. You can do this by going to File > New File > R Script. Save the new file in your My_Labs folder with the name lab_8.R. (If you named your folder something different, just save it there.)


## Load some practice data

We will use the small vdem data set

```
data_vdem <- read.csv(here("data", "vdemdata.csv"))
```

## Simple OLS regression, one variable

```
# Create the model object

model1 <- lm(gdp_per_capita ~ liberal_democracy, data = data_vdem)

# show a summary of the model

summary(model1)

# create a stargazer table to copy to Word
library(stargazer)
stargazer(model1, type = "text", title = "OLS Regression of GDP")


```

For practice, what is the equation for a line you would make from this?

## Two variables - X plus one control variable (X2)

```
model2 <- lm(gdp_per_capita ~ liberal_democracy + exports, data = data_vdem)

# show the summary
summary(model2)

# create stargazer table for publication

stargazer(model2, type = "text", title = "OLS Regression of GDP with Control Variable")


```

For practice, try turning this into an equation. Hint: you have to add the coefficient times X2 (exports) to the right hand side of the equation from model 1.


## Two variables, one categorical

First, we have to add a categorical variable to the data. We will use the "country" variable and turn it into a "factor".

```
# we have to turn the country variable into a factor
data_vdem$country <- as.factor(data_vdem$country)

# now we can run the model with the categorical variable
model3 <- lm(gdp_per_capita ~ liberal_democracy + country, data = data_vdem)

# show the summary
summary(model3)

# create stargazer table for publication
stargazer(model3, type = "text", title = "OLS Regression of GDP with Categorical Variable")

```

We actually get 178 coefficients for the country variable because there are 179 countries in the data and R automatically drops one of them as the "reference category". The coefficients for the country variable represent the difference in GDP per capita between each country and the reference category.

Since each country except the one we are interest in has a value of 0, all the coefficients drop except the one we are interested in and we get a clean equation for each country such as:

For United States:

$GDP_{per\_capita} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot liberal\_democracy + \beta_2 \cdot country\_United\_States$

Zimbabwe is the reference country, so its equation is just:

$GDP_{per\_capita} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot liberal\_democracy$

## Assignment

For the assignment, you will run a regression analysis for two variables in your data set. First, you need to decide which will be your X (explanatory, indepedent, or causal variable) and which will be your Y (outcome, dependent, or effect variable). You should have done this in a previous lab when you wrote a paragraph about your variables. Then, you will run a simple OLS regression with just those two variables. Next, you will add a control variable **if you have one** to the model and run it again. 

You should create tables for each model in Word. 

You should also write out the equations for each model in Word.

You should describe the effect of X on Y in the first model. You can use this as a template: "For each unit increase in [X], [Y] increases by [the value of the coefficient] for [X] in model 1, holding all else constant." The bracketed items need to actually match your variabe names and results.

Optional but encouraged!: 

Describe how the effect of X on Y changes when you add the control variable in model 2. You can use this as a template: "When we add the control variable to the model, the effect of X on Y changes to [describe how it changes]. This suggests that [describe what this means for your theory]."




# Credits

## Authorship and License

- Author: Tom Hanna
- Website: <a href="https://tom-hanna.org/">tomhanna.me</a>
- License: This work is licensed under a <a href="http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/">Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International License.</a>

- License: This work is licensed under a <a href= "http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/">Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International License.</>

<a href= "http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/">![Creative Commons License](`r here("assets", "creative_commons_license.png")`)</a>
